questions.passage:
Stand before almost any contemporary artwork in almost any museum and read the small panel beside it. The work, you will learn, 'interrogates' something — the body, the archive, the border. It 'complicates' a second thing, 'negotiates' a third, and 'occupies the liminal space' between two abstractions that were not, until that moment, known to have a space between them. The sentences are grammatical. The vocabulary is recognizably English. And a visitor of ordinary intelligence, reading with complete attention, will come away knowing almost nothing about what the object in front of her is, how it was made, or why anyone thought it worth making.

Defenders of the dialect say that difficult art demands difficult language, that plain words would flatten what is genuinely strange in the work. This gets the relationship exactly backward. Genuinely technical vocabularies earn their difficulty by compressing content. A chemist's term can always be unpacked: the paraphrase will be longer, clumsier, and perfectly precise. Try the same operation on a wall label. Ask what, concretely, it would mean for a stack of photographs to 'interrogate the archive,' and the paraphrase does not lengthen — it evaporates. The sentence was never a compressed claim. It was a gesture wearing the costume of a claim.

What, then, is the dialect for? Its function is social before it is semantic. Fluency in it certifies membership: the curator, the grant writer, and the graduate student can produce it on demand, and producing it on demand is how they show one another that they belong. And it performs a second service that is rarely acknowledged. A claim with no cash value cannot be tested against the object, so the label insulates both artwork and institution from the visitor's judgment. You cannot say the panel is wrong about the piece; the panel was never, in the relevant sense, about the piece at all.

The church once conducted its rites in a Latin the congregation could not follow, and the arrangement was not an oversight. A language valued for its opacity makes intermediaries indispensable: someone must stand between you and the mystery, and the standing is the point. Museum prose works the same way. Its obscurity is not a failure to communicate but a way of relocating authority — away from the eyes of the person in the room and toward the professionals who speak the tongue.

None of this is an argument that art writing must be simple, still less that it must be short. Plain description is in fact the harder discipline. Conservators manage it constantly: a condition report will tell you that a canvas has cupping paint along a diagonal crease, that a varnish has yellowed unevenly toward the lower left, and in telling you it will teach you to see. Such prose is exact, vivid, and falsifiable, which is to say it takes the risk that writing about objects ought to take. The wall label takes no risk, and that is why it teaches nothing.

I do not expect the institutions to reform themselves. Obscurity is serving them too well: it fills the space where evaluation would otherwise go, and it keeps the priesthood employed. But the visitor owes the dialect no deference. If you stand in a gallery, read a panel twice, and find that nothing has arrived, the failure is not yours. Your bafflement is information. It is telling you, accurately, that the text in front of you had nothing to say, and it is inviting you to do the one thing the label cannot: look at the object, and begin describing what is actually there.
questions.stem: Which of the following best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Contemporary art has become so conceptually demanding that no vocabulary, plain or technical, can adequately convey its intentions to the general museum public.
choices.B: Museum visitors should be offered formal instruction in the specialized vocabulary of curatorial writing before being expected to engage with contemporary art.
choices.C: The jargon of museum labels functions to certify insiders and shield institutions from judgment rather than to communicate about artworks.
choices.D: Museums should eliminate interpretive wall text altogether and allow every artwork to be encountered without any accompanying language.

explanations.A: The passage claims the opposite about the capacity of language: conservators' plain descriptions are held up as exact, vivid, and instructive, so adequate vocabulary exists. The failure lies in a particular dialect, not in the possibility of conveying art in words.
explanations.B: The author holds that the dialect has no compressed content to learn — paraphrase makes it 'evaporate' — so training visitors in it would certify them without informing them. The recommendation is to trust one's bafflement, not to acquire fluency.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage argues that the label's function is 'social before it is semantic': fluency certifies membership, and unfalsifiable claims insulate the institution from the visitor's judgment. The Latin comparison and the conservators' counterexample both serve this central claim.
explanations.D: The author explicitly denies arguing that art writing must be simple or short, and praises condition reports as prose that teaches the reader to see. The target is a specific evasive dialect, not the presence of interpretive text as such.

questions.stem: The author refers to the church conducting its rites in Latin primarily in order to:

choices.A: illustrate how a language valued for its opacity can make intermediaries indispensable and relocate authority.
choices.B: suggest that the reverence people feel toward contemporary art is best understood as a form of religious devotion.
choices.C: trace the historical origins of curatorial vocabulary to the ceremonial languages of early religious institutions.
choices.D: argue that older institutions communicated with their publics far more successfully than museums do today.

explanations.A: Correct. The paragraph states that the arrangement 'was not an oversight' and draws the parallel directly: opacity makes someone 'stand between you and the mystery,' and museum prose likewise relocates authority away from the visitor toward professionals who speak the tongue.
explanations.B: The comparison concerns the social mechanics of an opaque language, not the psychology of viewers. Nothing in the passage characterizes visitors' responses to art as devotion; the visitor is portrayed as baffled, not worshipful.
explanations.C: The passage draws a structural analogy between two arrangements; it never claims that curatorial vocabulary descended historically from liturgical language. The comparison is about function, not lineage.
explanations.D: The Latin rite is presented as another case of deliberate non-communication, not as a success story. Treating it as a model of effective outreach reverses the point of the comparison.

questions.stem: In the final paragraph, the claim that 'your bafflement is information' most nearly means that:

choices.A: confusion in a gallery signals that the visitor should return to the label and read it a third or fourth time with much greater care and patience.
choices.B: the discomfort of not understanding is itself the aesthetic experience the artwork was designed to produce in viewers.
choices.C: visitors who feel confused are detecting gaps in their own education that museums have an obligation to remedy.
choices.D: a reader's failure to extract meaning is accurate evidence about the emptiness of the text rather than a personal shortcoming.

explanations.A: The scenario in the paragraph already includes reading the panel twice with nothing arriving, and the author concludes 'the failure is not yours.' Prescribing further rereading treats the text as full and the reader as deficient, which the passage denies.
explanations.B: The bafflement at issue is produced by the wall text, not by the artwork; the author's closing advice is to turn away from the label and look at the object. Attributing the confusion to the art's design misassigns its source.
explanations.C: The author explicitly absolves the visitor: bafflement tells her 'the text in front of you had nothing to say.' Reading it as a diagnosis of the visitor's education inverts who is being judged.
explanations.D: Correct. The sentence reassigns the meaning of confusion: rather than indicating the reader's inadequacy, it accurately reports that the label lacked content. The bafflement 'is telling you, accurately,' something about the text — hence, information.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward those who defend the gallery dialect as suited to difficult art can best be described as:

choices.A: dismissive, treating their defense as an inversion of how genuinely technical language actually works.
choices.B: ambivalent, granting that their position succeeds for some artworks while failing for others.
choices.C: respectful, acknowledging the defense as serious before reluctantly setting it aside on practical grounds.
choices.D: curious, presenting their view as an open puzzle that the passage declines to resolve either way.

explanations.A: Correct. The author says the defense 'gets the relationship exactly backward' and demonstrates the reversal with the chemistry contrast: real technical terms unpack into precise paraphrase, while the label 'evaporates.' The defense is not weighed; it is overturned.
explanations.B: No artwork or label is exempted anywhere in the passage; the dialect is condemned wholesale as 'a gesture wearing the costume of a claim.' The author never grants the defenders partial success.
explanations.C: There is no moment of acknowledgment or reluctance. The rebuttal is immediate and total, and the defenders reappear later only as a 'priesthood' whose obscurity keeps them employed — hardly a respectful framing.
explanations.D: The passage resolves the matter emphatically rather than leaving it open: the dialect's function is declared to be social certification and insulation from judgment. Nothing is framed as an undecided puzzle.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings, if true, would most weaken the author's argument?

choices.A: A survey finds that most museum curators privately agree that wall labels are frequently overwritten and formulaic.
choices.B: An analysis shows that condition reports written by conservators contain more specialized terminology than wall labels do.
choices.C: Studies find that visitors who read standard interpretive panels can afterward describe the specific works accurately and in concrete detail.
choices.D: A historical study establishes that interpretive museum wall text was rare in most public galleries everywhere before the second half of the twentieth century.

explanations.A: Curators conceding that labels are overwritten is consistent with the author's account and even supports it; the argument does not depend on curators sincerely believing in the prose they produce.
explanations.B: The author's praise of condition reports rests on their being exact, vivid, and falsifiable, not on their avoiding specialized terms. A technical vocabulary that unpacks into precise paraphrase is exactly what the passage endorses.
explanations.C: Correct. The core claim is that a visitor reading with complete attention 'will come away knowing almost nothing' about the object because the text has no cash value. Evidence that readers of these panels gain accurate, concrete understanding of specific works would contradict that claim directly.
explanations.D: The recency of the practice says nothing about whether it communicates. The author's argument concerns the dialect's function, and a short history is fully compatible with that function being certification rather than communication.

questions.stem: Based on the passage's contrast between a chemist's terminology and wall-label language, the author would most likely classify which of the following alongside the wall label?

choices.A: A statute whose dense interlocking clauses can be expanded by any trained lawyer into long but perfectly exact statements of what conduct is required and what is forbidden.
choices.B: A corporate mission statement whose phrases grow vaguer, rather than longer and more precise, each time someone attempts to restate them concretely.
choices.C: A field guide whose compressed entries unfold into detailed identifying marks when a novice consults the glossary.
choices.D: A musical score whose notation can be rendered into sound by any performer trained in the conventions of the system.

explanations.A: Statutory language behaves like the chemist's term in the passage: the paraphrase is 'longer, clumsier, and perfectly precise.' Difficulty that compresses real content is precisely what the author exempts from criticism.
explanations.B: Correct. The diagnostic in the passage is what happens under paraphrase: genuine technical claims lengthen into precision, while the wall label 'evaporates.' Language whose restatement yields vagueness rather than content shares the label's defining failure — a gesture wearing the costume of a claim.
explanations.C: A guide entry that unfolds into detailed identifying marks is compression earning its difficulty, the pattern the passage attributes to legitimate technical vocabularies rather than to the gallery dialect.
explanations.D: Notation that trained performers can reliably cash out into sound has determinate content by the passage's own standard. The wall label's defect is that no such cashing-out is possible, so the score falls on the chemist's side of the contrast.
